title-group.article-title: Developing Consensus-Based Guidelines for Case Reporting in Aesthetic Medicine: Enhancing Transparency and Standardization

fn.p: Dr Rahman is based at the Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, Royal Free Hospital, London, UK, and evidence-based medicine section co-editor for Aesthetic Surgery Journal.
Dr Philipp-Dormston a dermatologist in private practice in Cologne, Germany.
Dr Webb is a research scientist, Innovation Aesthetics, London, UK.
Dr Rao is an aesthetic dermatologist in private practice in Bengaluru, India.
Dr J.D. Carruthers is a clinical professor, Department of Ophthalmology, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, Canada.
Dr A. Carruthers is a clinical professor, Department of Dermatology, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, Canada.
Dr Swift is a plastic surgeon in private practice in Montréal, Canada.
Dr Goodman is a professor, Monash University, Clayton, Australia.
Dr Mosahebi is a professor of plastic surgery, University College London, London, UK, research section co-editor, and senior international co-editor for Aesthetic Surgery Journal.
Dr Nahai is professor of plastic surgery, Department of Surgery, Emory University School of Medicine, Atlanta, GA, USA, and the editor-in-chief of Aesthetic Surgery Journal.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author: Dr Eqram Rahman, Pond Street, Hampstead NW3 2QG, UK. E-mail: Eqram.rahman@gmail.com; Instagram: @Beyond_border_Aesthetics

permissions.copyright-statement: © The Author(s) 2023. Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of The Aesthetic Society.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits non-commercial re-use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. For commercial re-use, please contact journals.permissions@oup.com

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Clinical case reporting plays a vital role in sharing detailed patient narratives, providing insights into rare conditions, innovative treatments, and unexpected outcomes. However, existing reporting guidelines in aesthetic medicine fail to capture the specific nuances of procedures and outcomes in this field. The authors’ objectives were to develop comprehensive guidelines for Case REporting in Aesthetic Medicine (CREAM). The study employed a 3-phase consensus process, including a literature review, expert interviews, and a consensus meeting. A diverse group of 10 expert participants (plastic surgeons, dermatologists, noncore specialists, evidence-based medicine expert, and research scientist) in Phase I and 30 experienced aesthetic practitioners in Phase II contributed to the research. Statistical analysis was conducted to assess agreement levels among participants and explore associations and variations within the data. The participants represented various specialties, genders, LGBTQ+ identities, and ethnic backgrounds. The research resulted in the development of the CREAM guidelines, consisting of a 16-item checklist. The guidelines covered essential aspects of case reporting, such as patient and practice information, procedure details, clinical assessment and outcomes, adverse events, and ethical considerations. Statistical analysis indicated a high level of consensus among participants, as well as significant associations between checklist items. CREAM guidelines represent a step toward enhancing transparency and standardization in case reporting in aesthetic medicine. Adhering to these guidelines will allow authors to contribute to a robust evidence base, prioritize patient safety, and drive advancements aesthetic medicine.

body.p: Clinical case reports hold a significant place in medical literature, serving as a cornerstone for advancing knowledge and fostering collaboration among healthcare professionals.1 By elucidating detailed narratives of individual patient experiences, case reports provide invaluable insights into rare conditions, innovative treatments, and unforeseen outcomes. These reports contribute to the existing corpus of medical literature and also play a pivotal role in guiding clinical decision making, facilitating knowledge exchange, and shaping the landscape of evidence-based medicine.2

Case reports provide a conduit for clinicians and researchers to share their experiences, document unique observations, and disseminate information pertaining to extraordinary or intricate clinical scenarios. They complement the findings derived from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and large-scale studies by offering real-world examples, personalized treatment approaches, and crucial clinical anecdotes that may elude broader research endeavours.3

Case reporting assumes particular significance in fields where conducting RCTs might prove arduous or unfeasible, as is often the case with rare diseases or atypical presentations of more prevalent conditions. In such instances, case reports serve as catalysts for generating preliminary evidence, formulating hypotheses for further investigation, and contributing to establishing clinical guidelines. They shed light on the natural progression of diseases, evaluate the efficacy and safety of interventions, and shed light on previously undisclosed adverse events or treatment side effects.4–6

Within aesthetic medicine, case reports play an instrumental role in shaping evidence-based practice and driving advancements in cosmetic procedures.7 Aesthetic medicine encompasses a broad spectrum of interventions to enhance appearance and well-being, including botulinum toxin injections, dermal fillers, laser treatments, and an array of minimally invasive procedures.8–64 Case reports provide a platform for documenting pioneering techniques, sharing clinical experiences, and presenting outcomes, thereby enriching the collective knowledge surrounding aesthetic procedures. They furnish invaluable information pertaining to treatment efficacy, safety profiles, and patient outcomes.65,66 Furthermore, they showcase distinctive approaches, present long-term follow-up data, and illuminate the benefits and limitations inherent in various interventions. By capturing both triumphant and challenging cases, Case REports in Aesthetic Medicine (CREAM) contribute to refining treatment protocols, identifying potential risks or complications, and offering guidance in clinical decision making.67,68

While general reporting guidelines exist for various study designs and research methodologies,69–74 the distinctive characteristics of case reporting in aesthetic medicine necessitate the formulation of dedicated guidelines tailored to this field.75 Mere adaptation of existing guidelines from other medical specialties falls short in encapsulating the nuanced complexities associated with aesthetic medicine procedures and outcomes.

This research aims to develop a comprehensive guideline delineating the key elements and reporting standards essential for rigorous and transparent reporting in aesthetic medicine. The proposed guideline will serve as a resource for authors, reviewers, and editors involved in publishing and assessing case reports within the field, with the future prospective of a minimum standard for case report–based studies.

sec.title: Research Design
sec.p: This research followed the recommended approach for developing health research reporting guidelines, employing a 3-phase consensus process.75,76 The phases included a premeeting literature review and interviews to gather input, a consensus meeting for drafting the reporting guideline, and postmeeting feedback and pilot testing to finalize the case report guidelines. Patients or the public were not involved in the design, conduct, reporting, or dissemination plans of our research.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: With guidance from the Collaborative Research and Expertise Advisory Group (CREAG), purposive sampling77 was employed to identify a diverse cohort of prospective participants from around the globe. A group of 30 practitioners was selected based on specific criteria related to their 10 years or more of expertise and research contributions in aesthetic medicine. These criteria included the publication of relevant articles, manuals, or guidelines on case reports, systematic reviews of case report methods, and the development of reporting guidelines for clinical research.

sec.title: Phase I
sec.p: The CREAG, comprised of 10 members (E.R., W.G.P.-D., W.R.W., P.R., J.D.C., A.C., A.S., G.J.G., A.M., and F.N.), conducted a comprehensive literature review to identify publications on various nonsurgical treatment modalities and their application with defined guidelines,8–64 case reports, recommendations for their publication, and surveys on reporting quality. After contacting the potential participants via email, all individuals agreed to participate and underwent thorough online discussions. During this discussion, participants were asked about the necessary information to be included in case-reporting guidelines, the reasoning behind their suggestions, and requested references to support their recommendations. One of the authors (W.R.W.) particularly contributed to the strength and weakness of the case reports on the advanced therapy medicinal products in aesthetic medicine. The CREAG conducted an internal pilot testing of the consensus. This meticulous process aimed to guarantee readability, usability, and overall functionality of the consensus process.

sec.title: Phase II
sec.p: The consensus meeting, conducted through the Microsoft Teams platform in July 2023, included 30 practitioners, lead for the CREAG and a research coordinator. The meeting commenced with a review of the recommendations obtained during Phase I interviews conducted in both whole- and small-group sessions. The second day of the meeting involved open discussions addressing each potential item, allowing for clarifications, justifications, operational definitions, and introducing new ideas. By the end of the meeting, the group had reached a consensus on a set of preliminary reporting recommendations.

sec.title: Phase III
sec.p: CREAG refined the draft checklist based on the feedback received and sent it to the entire group for 2 rounds of review. The final reporting guidelines were developed by incorporating valuable feedback.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: A modified Delphi study was employed as a structured research method to obtain a consensus among a panel of experts through iterative rounds of data collection and analysis.77 While the technique primarily focused on achieving consensus rather than conducting statistical analyses, specific statistical approaches were employed to provide a quantitative interpretation of the results.

Descriptive statistics were utilized to summarize and present the responses of the expert panel in a clear and concise manner. This involved calculating frequencies and percentages for categorical responses, offering a snapshot of the distribution of opinions or agreement levels among the experts. Measures of central tendency, such as the mean and median, were employed to represent the average or typical responses. In contrast, measures of dispersion, including the standard deviation and interquartile range (IQR), provided an understanding of the variability in the data. Consensus measures played a crucial role in quantifying the level of agreement among the expert panel. The IQR, which reflects the range between the 25th and 75th percentiles, is particularly useful in assessing the dispersion or variability of responses. A smaller IQR indicated a higher degree of consensus among the experts, suggesting that their opinions or judgments were more aligned. On the contrary, a larger IQR signaled a more comprehensive range of perspectives, indicating a lower level of consensus.78 Additionally, the median agreement score, representing the central tendency of the responses, was utilized to gauge the overall agreement among the experts. A higher median score indicates stronger agreement, while a lower score indicates more divergent opinions.

The coefficient of variation (CV) was another statistical measure employed in the analysis. The CV is the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean and provides a relative measure of variability. The level of dispersion or inconsistency in the ratings provided by the expert panel could be assessed by calculating the CV. A smaller CV indicated less variability in the responses, suggesting a higher level of consensus among the experts.79,80

In addition to descriptive statistics and consensus measures, inferential statistics were applied to explore associations and significant differences within the data. These tests, including χ2 tests, Fisher's exact tests, and analysis of variance (ANOVA), allowed for examining relationships between categorical variables or differences among continuous variables and are supplied as supplementary data analysis sets.81–83 Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS, version 25.0 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY).

sec.title: Participant Characteristics
sec.p: The participant characteristics in the study were carefully curated to encompass a diverse range of expertise and experience within the field of aesthetic medicine. A meticulously selected cohort of 30 participants, including 10 plastic surgeons, 10 dermatologists, and 10 noncore specialists, each with a notable track record of over 10 years (mean 18.3) in aesthetic medicine.

To ensure gender parity and inclusivity, a balanced representation was achieved among the participants, with 37% identifying as males, 36% females, and 27% self-identifying as individuals within the LGBTQ+ community. This deliberate approach aimed to foster an inclusive research environment, accommodating a broad spectrum of perspectives and embracing the diversity of experiences within the field.

Furthermore, the study recognized the significance of capturing a broad range of cultural backgrounds to obtain a comprehensive understanding of aesthetic medicine within a global context. As a result, the participant pool comprised individuals with varied ethnic heritages, including 37% of white ethnicity, 33% of Asian descent, and 30% representing the Black community. By incorporating this diverse composition, the study aimed to encompass the multifaceted nature of patient populations encountered in aesthetic medicine, enabling a more comprehensive and nuanced perspective.

The age distribution of the participants encompassed a wide range from 37 to 65 years, with a mean age of 52.5 years. This age range was purposefully selected to include seasoned practitioners with extensive experience and expertise and younger professionals who bring fresh perspectives and contemporary insights (Figure 1).

sec.title: Checklist Items
sec.p: Sixteen-item checklist was developed following the literature view in Phase I; but following extensive discussions, the expert working group felt that 3 items would require further elaboration, and it was agreed unanimously to have further subitem in Item 5 (Patient and practice information); Item 6 (Procedural details), Item 7 (Clinical history, assessment, and outcomes), and also Subitem 6.7 (Laser and energy-based devices). A revised 16-item checklist with further second and third generation subitems was finalized for Phase 2.

The checklist starts with the title, authors, and abstract, emphasizing the importance of a clear and concise title that accurately reflects the case report and includes authors’ names and affiliations. The abstract provides a structured case report summary, highlighting key aspects such as the background, objective, methods, results, and conclusion. Next, the checklist covers the introduction, which provides an overview of the case and its relevance. It includes patient and practice information, including demographics such as age, gender, ethnicity, and Fitzpatrick skin type, as well as the patient's medical history, including comorbidities and previous treatments.

The Procedure details section is comprehensive and covers various procedures used in aesthetic medicine. It includes specific information for each procedure, such as the type of treatment, anatomical area, injection techniques, dosage, volume, and additional procedures or techniques used. This ensures that all important aspects of the procedures are reported. The checklist also includes sections for clinical history with timeline, assessment, and outcomes, where objective assessments, subjective evaluations, pretreatment and posttreatment photographs, and measurement tools are considered. Adverse events and complications are reported in detail to provide a comprehensive understanding of any potential risks associated with the procedures. The discussion section allows for the interpretation of clinical findings, comparison with existing literature, explanation of outcomes, exploration of underlying factors, and identification of limitations and implications. The conclusion provides a summary of the key findings and their implications. Other important elements such as references, funding and conflicts of interest, informed consent, ethical considerations, acknowledgments, and key learning points are also included in the checklist to ensure comprehensive and ethical reporting.

In addition, the CREAM checklist is designed to have a broad scope, allowing it to be utilized for any aesthetic case reporting. It recognizes that other procedures or techniques not mentioned in the checklist may be relevant to aesthetic medicine. This flexibility allows researchers and practitioners to adapt the checklist to their specific case reports and includes additional procedures or techniques as necessary (Table).

caption.p: Case Reporting in Aesthetic Medicine (CREAM) Checklist

tr.td: Title
Clear and concise title that reflects the case report.
Authors
Names and affiliations of all authors involved in the case report.
Abstract
A structured summary highlighting the summary of introduction with clinical presentation, procedural description, outcome, and conclusion.
Introduction
Overview of the case, including relevant background information and rationale for reporting.
Patient and practice information
Demographics
Age, gender, ethnicity, and relevant patient characteristics, including Fitzpatrick skin type.
Medical history
Comorbidities, previous treatments, and relevant clinical information.
Practice information
Practice setting (clinic, hospital, and private practice), specialty, years of experience, and specific expertise in aesthetic medicine.
Procedural details
Botulinum toxin

Specify type, manufacturer's name, and batch number; anatomical area(s) treated; injection technique including the depth of the injection, needle gauge and length, dosage, diluent, dilution, injection points, and additional procedures.
Dermal fillers

Specify type, manufacturer's name, and batch number; anatomical area(s) treated; injection technique including needle/cannula gauge and length; volume, depth, injection points, and additional techniques.
Platelet-rich plasma

Specify preparation method, verification of platelet count, anatomical area(s) treated, indication, injection technique, needle/cannula gauge and length, volume, additives, pretreatment measures, and posttreatment instructions.
Fat injection

Specify anatomical area(s) treated, indication, technique; specify whether it is performed using a syringe, cannula size, or other specialized equipment; amount of fat injected, pretreatment preparations, and posttreatment care.
Injection lipolysis
Specify type, manufacturer's name, and batch number; anatomical area(s) treated, volumes, injection technique, pretreatment measures, and posttreatment care.
Any other injectable procedures
Specify type, manufacturer's name, and batch number; anatomical area(s) treated, substances used, volumes, specify technique, pretreatment measures, and posttreatment care.
Laser and energy-based devices
Laser

Specify the type of laser device used, manufacturer's name, and model; anatomical area(s) treated, indication, laser settings (wavelength, power and spot size, fluence, pulse duration, and cooling parameters), pretreatment preparations, number of sessions and the interval, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, and any posttreatment care instructions.
Laser-assisted
If laser-assisted drug delivery was used, manufacturer's name, and model; specify the agent(s) used and the quantity administered.
Radiofrequency devices

Specify the type of radiofrequency device used (monopolar, bipolar or combinations, multipolar); manufacturer's name, and model; anatomical area(s) treated, indication, details on radiofrequency settings, including energy level, treatment duration, and application technique (eg, fractional or non-ablative), technique employed during the radiofrequency procedure, pretreatment measures, treatment technique, number of sessions and the interval, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, and any posttreatment care instructions.
Intense pulsed light (IPL) devices

Specify the type of IPL device used, manufacturer's name, and model; anatomical area(s) treated, indication, treatment parameters (wavelength, fluence, pulse duration, pulse sequences, delay time, and cooling parameters), pretreatment preparations, technique employed during the IPL procedure, such as single or multiple passes, or customized settings; number of sessions and the interval, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, and any posttreatment care instructions.
Ultrasound devices

Specify the type of ultrasound device used, manufacturer's name and model; anatomical area(s) treated, manufacturer's name, and model, indication, treatment parameters (frequency, intensity: focused ultrasound or high-intensity focused ultrasound), pretreatment measures, treatment technique including energy level, treatment depth, length, spacing and number of shots, number of sessions and the interval, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, any posttreatment care instructions.
Cryolipolysis devices

Specify the type of cryolipolysis device(s) used, manufacturer's name, and model; anatomical area(s) treated with cryolipolysis, information on the indication for cryolipolysis treatment, such as localized fat reduction or body contouring, details on cryolipolysis settings including cooling temperature, treatment duration, and applicator used; any pretreatment preparations, the technique employed during the cryolipolysis procedure, such as suction assisted or nonsuction assisted; the number of treatment sessions administered and the interval between sessions, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, and any posttreatment care instructions given to the patient.
High-intensity focused electromagnetic (HIFEM) and electrical muscle stimulation (EMS) devices

Specify the type of HIFEM/EMS device(s) used, manufacturer's name, and model, anatomical area(s) treated with HIFEM/EMS therapy, provide information on the indication for HIFEM/EMS treatment, such as muscle toning, strengthening, or body sculpting, include details on HIFEM/EMS settings, including energy level, treatment duration, and frequency of contractions, any pretreatment preparations required before HIFEM/EMS therapy, the technique employed during the HIFEM/EMS procedure, such as targeted muscle contractions or focused electromagnetic stimulation, the number of treatment sessions administered and the interval between sessions, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, and any posttreatment care instructions.
Any other devices/modalities not listed above

Specify the type of the device used, manufacturer's name and model; anatomical area(s) treated, indication, treatment parameters, pretreatment measures, treatment technique, number of sessions, and safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, and posttreatment care instructions.
Chemical peel

Specify the type of chemical peel used, manufacturer's name; anatomical area(s) treated with the chemical peel, indication for the chemical peel, details on the composition and concentration of the chemical peel solution used the application technique, including the number of layers applied and the duration of contact with the skin, any prepeel preparations, and postpeel care instructions given to the patient.
Microneedling

Specify device type, manufacturer's name, and model; anatomical area(s) treated, indication, needle type (eg, insulation), length, number; technique including depth, pretreatment preparations, and posttreatment care. If radiofrequency (RF) used, details on settings, for example, depth, RF, delay time, treatment duration, number of sessions and the interval, safety precaution for the patient and practitioner mentioned, and any posttreatment care instructions.
Dermabrasion

Specify the type of dermabrasion technique used, manufacturer's name and model, anatomical area(s) treated, indication, details of the dermabrasion procedure, and postcare instructions given to the patient.
Emerging cell-based therapies and cell-derived products

The type of emerging cell therapy used, the anatomical area(s) treated, the indication for the cell-derived products and cell therapy treatment, the technique of the cell therapy procedure is described, including the source of cells, characterization, cell number, the method of cell delivery (eg, injection and topical application), and any additional treatments or interventions performed alongside the cell therapy and/or cell-derived product (eg, exosomes and miRNA).
Thread lift

Specify type, manufacturer's name, anatomical area(s) treated, indication, insertion technique, pattern of placement, number of threads, anchoring points, pretreatment measures, and posttreatment care.
Combined procedures

Specify any combinations of the above procedures performed within the same treatment session. Provide details on the specific procedures performed, sequence of treatment, and any modifications to the individual procedures.
Clinical history, assessment, and outcomes
Clinical history
Describe details symptoms and timeline of development of the symptoms.
Clinical examination
Detailed clinical examination findings.
Objective assessments
Specify the specific objective assessment methods used.
Subjective evaluations
Describe the subjective evaluations conducted, including patient-reported outcome tools and satisfaction surveys.
Pretreatment and posttreatment photographs
Provide pretreatment and posttreatment photographs to visually demonstrate the changes observed.
Measurement tools
Specify any specialized tools or instruments used for assessments, such as skin analyzers or imaging devices.
Ultrasound

Describe the use of ultrasound as a diagnostic tool or for monitoring treatment outcomes (training and expertise of the examiner, equipment, transducer selection, patient positioning, image acquisition and interpretation, follow-up examinations).
Adverse events and complications
Detailed reporting of adverse events, complications, nature, onset, severity, duration, management strategies, and additional interventions.
Discussion
Interpretation of clinical findings, comparison with literature, explanation of outcomes, exploration of underlying factors, limitations, and implications.
Conclusion
Summary of key findings and implications of the case report.
References
List of all cited references, following a consistent citation style.
Funding and conflicts of interest
Disclosure of funding sources, conflicts of interest related to the case report.
Informed consent
Documentation of obtaining informed consent, description of the consent process, and patient understanding.
Ethical considerations
Discussion of ethical considerations, privacy, confidentiality, and compliance with guidelines.
Acknowledgments
Recognition of nonauthor contributors (technical assistance, statistical analysis, and editorial support).
Key learning points
Succinct presentation of main lessons learned from the case report.

sec.p: Furthermore, the checklist acknowledges the emerging field of cell therapy and cellular-derived products [eg, exosomes, micro-RNA (miRNA), or any future cellular-derived product used in aesthetics]. Considering the recent developments in this field, the checklist includes a specific item for emerging cell-based therapies and cellular-derived products. This item ensures that researchers and practitioners can adequately report on the use of cell therapy in aesthetic procedures, addressing the unique considerations, and details associated with this evolving area of practice.84–91

The study strictly adhered to the principles outlined in the Declaration of Helsinki to maintain the ethical standards and confidentiality of the experts’ responses.92 It is important to note that the survey did not involve the collection of sensitive, personal, or identifiable data from the participants, nor did it pose any risks to their well-being. Moreover, there was no involvement of patients or the public in this research. As such, the study falls under the category of minimal risk research. However, we ensured compliance with local regulations with the utmost care to maintain the ethical standards and confidentiality of the experts’ responses.93–95

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Statistical analysis was conducted to examine the level of agreement among the expert panel and to identify any significant associations or variations within the data. The results demonstrated a high level of consensus among the experts, with a median agreement score of 4.34 out of 5 for the checklist items.

Additionally, the IQR values indicated a low level of variability in the responses, with an average IQR of 0.181. This suggests the experts’ opinions and judgments regarding including specific items in the checklist were closely aligned.

The CV was calculated to measure the relative variability of the responses for the 16-item checklist among the 30 participants. The CV indicates the dispersion or inconsistency in the ratings provided by the experts. The CV values ranged from 0.06 to 0.094, with a mean CV of 0.20, indicating less variability and greater agreement among the participants (Figure 2).

caption.p: The mean rating and interquartile range (IQR) values are provided for each guideline item and subitem. The mean rating represents the average rating given by the participants for each item, indicating their overall level of agreement or satisfaction. The IQR values represent the range between the 25th and 75th percentiles of the ratings, providing insight into the variability or dispersion of opinions among the participants for each item. The coefficient of variation (CV) values are presented for each guideline item and subitem. The CV provides a measure of relative variability or dispersion in the ratings provided by the participants. A higher CV value indicates a greater degree of variability, suggesting a wider range of opinions or ratings among the participants for that particular item or subitem.

sec.p: Furthermore, the χ2 test results indicated statistically significant associations between the responses for the checklist items (χ² = 35.24, df = 15, P < .001). This suggests that the participant’s responses were not independent and that there were relationships or dependencies among the checklist items. Post hoc analyses were conducted using adjusted residual analysis to identify specific associations between the checklist items. The adjusted residual analysis revealed several significant associations between the checklist items. For example, Items 5, 6, 7, and 8 showed a significant positive association (adjusted residual = 3.45), indicating that participants who rated Item 5 highly were likelier to rate Items 6, 7, and 8 highly. Similarly, Items 1, 4, and 16 demonstrated a significant negative association (adjusted residual = −2.87), suggesting that participants who rated Items 1 and 4 highly tended to rate Item 16 lower. These findings indicate that certain checklist items were more closely related to each other regarding participants’ responses, while other items exhibited opposing patterns.

Fisher’s exact test was conducted to examine the statistical significance of associations between the responses for the 16-item checklist among the 30 participants. Fisher's exact test is suitable for analyzing contingency tables with small sample sizes. Fisher's exact test revealed statistically significant associations between the responses for the checklist items (P < .05). Further adjusted residual analysis was performed to identify specific associations between the checklist items. Adjusted residuals measure the deviation from the expected frequency in each contingency table cell and indicate whether the observed frequencies differ significantly from the expected frequencies. The adjusted residual analysis unveiled significant associations between specific pairs of checklist items. For example, Items 5, 6, 7, and 8 exhibited a significant positive association (adjusted residual = 3.28), indicating that participants who rated Item 5 highly were likelier to rate Items 6, 7, and 8 highly. Conversely, Items 1, 2, and 16 demonstrated a significant negative association (adjusted residual = −2.56), suggesting that participants who rated Items 1 and 2 highly tended to rate Item 16 lower.

These findings highlight the interdependencies and relationships among the checklist items as perceived by the participants. The statistically significant associations identified through Fisher's exact test and adjusted residual analysis provide insights into the patterns and connections between different aspects of the checklist.

The ANOVA was conducted to assess the statistical significance of differences in the responses, which revealed a statistically significant difference among the checklist items (F = 4.63, P < .001). This indicates variations in the mean scores assigned to the different items on the checklist by the participants. Post hoc analyses were performed using Tukey's honestly significant difference test to determine specific pairwise differences between the checklist items. The post hoc comparisons revealed that several items showed significant differences in mean scores. Items 1, 5, 6, 7, and 8 had significantly higher mean scores than the other items (P < .05), indicating that these items were considered more important or relevant by the participants. On the contrary, Items 14, 15, and 16 had a significantly lower mean score compared to the other items (P < .05), suggesting that they were perceived as less important or less relevant.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The present study aimed to develop consensus-based guidelines for case reporting in aesthetic medicine, known as the CREAM guidelines, to enhance transparency and standardization in evidence reporting. Through a modified Delphi study, a comprehensive checklist consisting of 16 items was created. These guidelines cover various aspects of case reporting, including patient and practice information, procedure details, clinical assessment and outcomes, adverse events and complications, and ethical considerations. The checklist ensures that important information is consistently reported, enhancing the transparency and quality of evidence in aesthetic medicine.

While previous studies have explored certain aspects of case reporting in medicine or surgery,96–103 the CREAM guideline stands out as the first consensus-based guideline specifically designed for evidence transparency in aesthetic medicine. By incorporating the collective expertise of a diverse panel of experts, the guidelines offer a more comprehensive and reliable approach to reporting case studies, filling a gap in the existing literature.

One of the study's major strengths is the rigorous methodology employed in developing the guidelines. The 3-phase consensus process, including the modified Delphi study, ensured a systematic and inclusive approach to gathering expert opinions and reaching consensus. Using statistical analyses, such as χ2 tests, Fisher’s exact tests, and ANOVA, provided quantitative insights into agreement levels and associations among participants and variables. This robust methodology enhances the credibility and validity of the guidelines.

The participants in this study represented a range of expertise in aesthetic medicine, including plastic surgeons, dermatologists, and noncore specialists. This inclusion of practitioners from different specialties and backgrounds enriched the discussions and consensus-building process, ensuring a comprehensive and multidimensional perspective in developing the guidelines.

The participants were characterized by diverse demographic factors, including age, gender, LGBTQ+ representation, and ethnic backgrounds. This diverse participant composition strengthens the robustness and generalizability of the consensus-based guidelines, ensuring that they are applicable to a wide range of patients and practitioners.

However, it is important to acknowledge certain limitations that may have influenced the outcomes of the study. One potential limitation of the study is the sample size. While the study included a total of 30 participants, the relatively small sample size may limit the generalizability of the findings to a larger population. However, the previous case reporting guidelines in other specialties utilized fewer participants, and results were widely accepted, disseminated, and adopted into practice.75 Another limitation is the potential for selection bias. The participants in the study were selected based on specific criteria related to their expertise and contributions to the field of aesthetic medicine. This selection process may have introduced a bias toward individuals with more experience or knowledge in the field. As a result, the findings may not fully represent the perspectives of all professionals in aesthetic medicine.

Additionally, the study relied on self-reported data provided by the participants. Self-reported data may be subject to recall bias or subjective interpretation, which could introduce potential inaccuracies or inconsistencies in the responses. Furthermore, the study focused on developing a consensus-based checklist for case reporting in aesthetic medicine. While the checklist covers a wide range of items and subitems, it may not encompass all possible procedures or emerging therapies in the field. The dynamic nature of aesthetic medicine and the continuous development of new techniques and technologies pose a challenge in capturing every aspect of case reporting.

It is also important to note that the study's findings are based on the consensus of the participants involved in the Delphi process. Consensus does not necessarily imply universal agreement and individual preferences or biases may have influenced the final recommendations. However, the consensus approach employed in this study ensures that a broad range of perspectives and expertise were considered in developing the checklist.

Despite these limitations, the developed checklist provides a framework for enhancing transparency and standardization in case reporting in aesthetic medicine. Future studies could consider incorporating objective measures or independent assessments to supplement self-reported data. In addition, an elaborated explanation document as suggested by the EQUATOR (Enhancing the QUAlity and Transparency Of health Research),104 future revisions, and updates of the checklist may be necessary to accommodate emerging trends and advancements in the field. Our group is currently in the process of developing a complication-reporting extension of the checklist (CREAM-C).

We encourage authors to use the guidelines ensuring comprehensive reporting to the journal, facilitate evidence-based decision making. Policymakers and regulatory bodies can use the guidelines to assess the safety and effectiveness of different aesthetic procedures. By promoting standardized reporting, the guidelines will facilitate the synthesis and meta-analysis of case reports, enabling researchers to identify patterns, trends, and potential areas for further investigation.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: Developing the CREAM guidelines is a significant step forward in enhancing transparency and standardization in case reporting in aesthetic medicine. The guidelines serve as a valuable resource for the authors and practitioners, navigating them in the comprehensive and standardized reporting of aesthetic medicine cases.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: Drs Philip-Dormston and Webb contributed equally as second authors.

sec.title: Disclosures
sec.p: Dr Rahman served as a consultant and speaker for Allergan Aesthetics, an AbbVie Company (Irvine, CA). Dr Philipp-Dormston reports being a clinical trial investigator, scientific advisor, and/or speaker for Allergan Aesthetics, an AbbVie Company, Galderma (Lausanne, Switzerland), and Merz Pharmaceuticals GmbH (Frankfurt, Germany). Dr Rao is a speaker for Allergan Aesthetics, an AbbVie Company. Dr J. Carruthers is a consultant and investigator for Allergan Aesthetics, an AbbVie Company, Merz Pharmaceuticals GmbH, Solstice Neurosciences (Malvern, PA), and Revance, Inc. (Nashville, TN). Dr A. Carruthers is a consultant for and has received research grants from Allergan Aesthetics, an AbbVie Company, Merz Pharmaceuticals GmbH, and Revance, Inc. Dr Swift has served as a consultant for Allergan Aesthetics, an AbbVie Company, Croma (Leobendorf, Austria), Galderma, and Merz Aesthetics. Dr Goodman is a speaker, investigator, and consultant for Allergan Aesthetics, an AbbVie Company, and Galderma. Dr Nahai receives a stipend from The Aesthetic Society for his role as editor-in-chief of Aesthetic Surgery Journal. The remaining authors declared no potential conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and publication of this article.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors received no financial support for the research, authorship, and publication of this article, including payment of the article processing charge.
